items.definition: Whether the storyboard establishes the specific inn early through the lane, gate, signboard, courtyard door, reception touchpoint, or room door instead of starting with context-free decor or room mood shots.
high_score_signals: A real identity anchor appears within the first three shots
Exterior identity clearly connects to the room or courtyard footage
Viewers can quickly tell the property is specific, real, and findable
low_score_signals: The opening is only lanterns, tea, bedding, or old-house decor
Property identity appears only in text overlays
The outside and inside scenes have no verifiable relationship
items.score_rule: Earlier, clearer, and more property-specific identity proof earns a higher score.

items.feature: Cover alignment with the inn's core promise
items.definition: Whether the cover direction clearly answers the inn's main conversion reason, such as old-town slow stay, courtyard pause, calm arrival, morning closure, or reset travel rhythm, instead of drifting into generic boutique beauty.
high_score_signals: The title and hero frame point to one specific stay reason
The cover scene naturally leads into the first shots
The viewer can infer why this inn is worth staying in, not just looking at
low_score_signals: The cover only shows mood objects or room styling with no clear offer
The title promises arrival ease, breakfast, or local life that the cover does not support
The cover aesthetic feels more luxurious or more private than the real inn
items.score_rule: The cover scores higher when it uses real shootable proof to express the inn's true stay value.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard honestly visualizes how guests reach the inn and room, including lane turns, parking transfer, stairs, stone paths, bridges, slopes, or check-in guidance, instead of masking friction while promising convenience.
high_score_signals: The arrival chain from route anchor to inn entry is understandable
Stairs, path texture, or transfer logic are handled honestly
Convenience claims stay aligned with what the camera can really prove
low_score_signals: The plan implies car-to-door access with no proof
Important arrival friction is edited away while still being promised as easy
Route proof depends on maps or captions instead of real footage
items.score_rule: More verifiable and less exaggerated arrival logic earns a higher score.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard stays loyal to one real room type and proves bed, window, seating, luggage, and bathroom relationships instead of constructing a fantasy room from mixed assets or premium upgrades.
high_score_signals: One room type is consistently represented through the sequence
Bed, bathroom, window, and utility shots feel structurally coherent
Special features like terrace, tub, or view are shown only when real
low_score_signals: Multiple room types are cut together as if they were one stay
Only tight aesthetic shots appear and scale remains unclear
The script promises a standard room while the visuals quietly show an upgrade
items.score_rule: Room proof scores higher when it is structurally consistent and close to a real guest perspective.

items.definition: Whether public or semi-public spaces such as courtyards, rooftops, terraces, tea tables, or riverside decks are shown as real usable spaces with clear ownership and use boundaries.
high_score_signals: The full space is visible before detail inserts
The storyboard shows real use, not just static beauty
The relationship between the space and the inn is clear
low_score_signals: The space is presented only through closeups and could belong anywhere
A shared area is framed as a private guest entitlement
Night access or permission is assumed without confirmation
items.score_rule: Shared-space proof scores higher when viewers can understand what the space is, how it is used, and whether it truly belongs to the inn offer.

items.definition: When the script highlights host warmth, local guidance, welcome tea, key handoff, pickup support, or check-in ease, the storyboard should contain real, permissioned service proof instead of actor-style impressions.
high_score_signals: Real host or process evidence appears in the arrival flow
Human warmth is tied to a specific action such as greeting, guiding, or handing over access
The plan respects privacy while still proving service capability
low_score_signals: Friendly faces appear with no actual service context
Service claims exist only in narration or captions
The storyboard depends on unapproved people or invented host moments
items.score_rule: More specific and more permission-safe service proof earns a higher score.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard can credibly complete an inn stay arc into the next morning through breakfast, tea, first-walk rhythm, or morning light rather than ending with an unearned promise of overnight completeness.
high_score_signals: Morning scenes connect clearly back to the room, courtyard, or gate
Breakfast or morning use is tied to actual access windows
The overnight flow feels complete and executable
low_score_signals: The script promises breakfast or dawn payoff with no morning assets
Morning scenes are generic nature inserts with no inn connection
The stay arc feels cut off because only daytime or only nighttime assets exist
items.score_rule: The more executable and inn-linked the morning closure is, the higher the score.

items.definition: When the inn sells old-town life, riverside nights, market mornings, or village walks, the storyboard should prove that the surrounding scene and the inn entrance belong to one continuous stay experience.
high_score_signals: A nearby local anchor is visibly linked to the inn gate or return path
Outside footage supports a precise stay rhythm instead of generic destination hype
Location claims are conservative when exact distance is not provable
low_score_signals: Beautiful town or mountain footage appears with no relationship to the inn
Claims like 'right downstairs' or 'steps away' lack route evidence
Destination B-roll does all the emotional work while the inn stays unproven
items.score_rule: Local-life positioning scores higher when it is physically tied back to the inn itself.

items.feature: Shot feasibility under inn operating constraints
items.definition: Whether the shot plan matches real inn constraints such as limited staff, mixed-use spaces, guest privacy, low-light access, neighborhood sensitivity, breakfast hours, room availability, and short shoot windows.
high_score_signals: Key shots line up with confirmed time windows and permissions
A small crew can realistically finish the plan without disrupting operations
The plan does not rely on unapproved guests, impossible lighting, or long schedule expansion
low_score_signals: The storyboard assumes every room, space, and person is available on demand
A short shoot window still tries to cover full evening-to-morning production with no tradeoffs
The plan would obviously interrupt real inn operations
items.score_rule: More realistic shot ambition and stronger dependency fit earn a higher score.

items.definition: Whether the storyboard precisely lists what is needed for gate proof, host proof, night access, courtyard use, breakfast timing, room type access, and local-scene linkage, then clearly downgrades when those assets are missing.
high_score_signals: Each key story beat maps to a concrete asset or permission
Missing assets trigger a visible downgrade route and lower feasibility score
The plan never hides the difference between shootable proof and wished-for atmosphere
low_score_signals: Dependencies are vague, such as 'need inn footage' with no detail
Major gaps still produce a confident full-stay storyboard
Fallback paths are unclear and execution risk is pushed downstream
items.score_rule: Specific dependency mapping and conservative downgrade logic earn a higher score.
